It has been identified with "Wakeoe Shrine" of Yamada District, Owari Province, in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' but there are differing views as described below. === History === Until the {{jalink|Edo period}} it was called '''Rokusho Daimyōjin''' and was the {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} of Yasui village in {{jalink|Kasugai District|Q11513576}}. The early-Edo '{{jalink|Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629}}' records "Yasui village ... Rokusho Daimyōjin ... previously tax-exempt." At that time, the Inagaki family served as {{jalink|kannushi|Q15888735}}. Founding date is unknown. One theory says it originally stood at Senbonsugi to the northeast and was moved to the current site in {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} 12 ({{jalink|1584|Q6728}}) by order of {{jalink|Oda Nobukatsu|Q3044815}}, while another says shrine buildings had long stood around the present site because remains of dead Senbonsugi trees are within the precinct{{Sfn|加藤宏|1988|p=281}}. It is said to have lost part of shrine lands due to Bizen land surveys in the {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} period{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. In early {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} it was renamed "Wakeoe Shrine." It was burned in the {{jalink|1945|Q5240}} air raids ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 20) but rebuilt in {{jalink|1966|Q2649}} (Shōwa 41). The Hachiman shrine merged here, also called "Enna Hachiman" or "Engi Hachiman," is said to have originally stood in the same precinct{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. According to shrine tradition, it was founded at Senbonsugi in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Tenji|Q314802}}, but was heavily damaged by flood in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 17 ({{jalink|1520|Q6284}}){{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. It is said Asano Matazaemon Nagakatsu rebuilt it in {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} 11 ({{jalink|1583|Q6726}}), and a ridge tag from that time reportedly reads: "Tenshō 11 ... invited Hachiman-sha ... shrine official Inagaki Udayū Fujiwara Yasuo ... built by Asano Hachirōzaemon, nephew of Asano Nagamasa, descendant of Genji of Hachiman"{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. However, Edo-period gazetteers do not describe this Hachiman shrine. It was formally merged in {{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 9 ({{jalink|1920|Q2155}}) as a precinct shrine of Wakeoe Shrine{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. The Yamada District "Wakeoe Shrine" in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}' is the same as "Ōe Tenjin" in the '{{jalink|Owari Kuninai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830}}.' By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{jalink|Owari-shi|Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{jalink|old document|Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{jalink|Kōji|Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{jalink|Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843}} during construction of {{jalink|Nagoya Castle|Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{jalink|List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{osm box|w|454603831}} '''Wakeoe Shrine''' (わけおえじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Yasui 4-chōme, {{jalink|Kita-ku (Nagoya)|Q1207964|Kita-ku}}, {{jalink|Nagoya|Q11751}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. It has been identified with "Wakeoe Shrine" of Yamada District, Owari Province, in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' but there are differing views as described below. === History === Until the {{jalink|Edo period}} it was called '''Rokusho Daimyōjin''' and was the {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} of Yasui village in {{jalink|Kasugai District|Q11513576}}. The early-Edo '{{jalink|Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629}}' records "Yasui village ... Rokusho Daimyōjin ... previously tax-exempt." At that time, the Inagaki family served as {{jalink|kannushi|Q15888735}}. Founding date is unknown. One theory says it originally stood at Senbonsugi to the northeast and was moved to the current site in {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} 12 ({{jalink|1584|Q6728}}) by order of {{jalink|Oda Nobukatsu|Q3044815}}, while another says shrine buildings had long stood around the present site because remains of dead Senbonsugi trees are within the precinct{{Sfn|加藤宏|1988|p=281}}. It is said to have lost part of shrine lands due to Bizen land surveys in the {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} period{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. In early {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} it was renamed "Wakeoe Shrine." It was burned in the {{jalink|1945|Q5240}} air raids ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 20) but rebuilt in {{jalink|1966|Q2649}} (Shōwa 41). The Hachiman shrine merged here, also called "Enna Hachiman" or "Engi Hachiman," is said to have originally stood in the same precinct{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. According to shrine tradition, it was founded at Senbonsugi in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Tenji|Q314802}}, but was heavily damaged by flood in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 17 ({{jalink|1520|Q6284}}){{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. It is said Asano Matazaemon Nagakatsu rebuilt it in {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} 11 ({{jalink|1583|Q6726}}), and a ridge tag from that time reportedly reads: "Tenshō 11 ... invited Hachiman-sha ... shrine official Inagaki Udayū Fujiwara Yasuo ... built by Asano Hachirōzaemon, nephew of Asano Nagamasa, descendant of Genji of Hachiman"{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. However, Edo-period gazetteers do not describe this Hachiman shrine. It was formally merged in {{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 9 ({{jalink|1920|Q2155}}) as a precinct shrine of Wakeoe Shrine{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. The Yamada District "Wakeoe Shrine" in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}' is the same as "Ōe Tenjin" in the '{{jalink|Owari Kuninai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830}}.' By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{jalink|Owari-shi|Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{jalink|old document|Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{jalink|Kōji|Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{jalink|Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843}} during construction of {{jalink|Nagoya Castle|Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{jalink|List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It has been identified with "Wakeoe Shrine" of Yamada District, Owari Province, in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' but there are differing views as described below. === History === Until the {{ill||lt=Edo period|UNKNOWN}} it was called '''Rokusho Daimyōjin''' and was the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} of Yasui village in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576}}. The early-Edo '{{ill|lt=Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}' records "Yasui village ... Rokusho Daimyōjin ... previously tax-exempt." At that time, the Inagaki family served as {{ill|lt=kannushi|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}}. Founding date is unknown. One theory says it originally stood at Senbonsugi to the northeast and was moved to the current site in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1584|Q6728|WD=Q6728}}) by order of {{ill|lt=Oda Nobukatsu|Q3044815|WD=Q3044815}}, while another says shrine buildings had long stood around the present site because remains of dead Senbonsugi trees are within the precinct{{Sfn|加藤宏|1988|p=281}}. It is said to have lost part of shrine lands due to Bizen land surveys in the {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} period{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. In early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} it was renamed "Wakeoe Shrine." It was burned in the {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}} air raids ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20) but rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1966|Q2649|WD=Q2649}} (Shōwa 41). The Hachiman shrine merged here, also called "Enna Hachiman" or "Engi Hachiman," is said to have originally stood in the same precinct{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. According to shrine tradition, it was founded at Senbonsugi in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenji|Q314802|WD=Q314802}}, but was heavily damaged by flood in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1520|Q6284|WD=Q6284}}){{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. It is said Asano Matazaemon Nagakatsu rebuilt it in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1583|Q6726|WD=Q6726}}), and a ridge tag from that time reportedly reads: "Tenshō 11 ... invited Hachiman-sha ... shrine official Inagaki Udayū Fujiwara Yasuo ... built by Asano Hachirōzaemon, nephew of Asano Nagamasa, descendant of Genji of Hachiman"{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. However, Edo-period gazetteers do not describe this Hachiman shrine. It was formally merged in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155}}) as a precinct shrine of Wakeoe Shrine{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. The Yamada District "Wakeoe Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is the same as "Ōe Tenjin" in the '{{ill|lt=Owari Kuninai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}.' By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{ill|lt=Owari-shi|Q137855962|WD=Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{ill|lt=old document|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{ill|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843|WD=Q1069843}} during construction of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254|WD=Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Rokusho-shi | 札所等 = | 本殿 = | 例祭 = October 11 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Nagoya|label=Wakeoe
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{osm box|w|454603831}} '''Wakeoe Shrine''' (わけおえじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Yasui 4-chōme, {{ill|lt=Kita-ku (Nagoya)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Nagoya|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It has been identified with "Wakeoe Shrine" of Yamada District, Owari Province, in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' but there are differing views as described below. === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|UNKNOWN}} it was called '''Rokusho Daimyōjin''' and was the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} of Yasui village in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576}}. The early-Edo '{{ill|lt=Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}' records "Yasui village ... Rokusho Daimyōjin ... previously tax-exempt." At that time, the Inagaki family served as {{ill|lt=kannushi|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}}. Founding date is unknown. One theory says it originally stood at Senbonsugi to the northeast and was moved to the current site in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1584|Q6728|WD=Q6728}}) by order of {{ill|lt=Oda Nobukatsu|Q3044815|WD=Q3044815}}, while another says shrine buildings had long stood around the present site because remains of dead Senbonsugi trees are within the precinct{{Sfn|加藤宏|1988|p=281}}. It is said to have lost part of shrine lands due to Bizen land surveys in the {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} period{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. In early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} it was renamed "Wakeoe Shrine." It was burned in the {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}} air raids ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20) but rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1966|Q2649|WD=Q2649}} (Shōwa 41). The Hachiman shrine merged here, also called "Enna Hachiman" or "Engi Hachiman," is said to have originally stood in the same precinct{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. According to shrine tradition, it was founded at Senbonsugi in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenji|Q314802|WD=Q314802}}, but was heavily damaged by flood in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1520|Q6284|WD=Q6284}}){{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. It is said Asano Matazaemon Nagakatsu rebuilt it in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1583|Q6726|WD=Q6726}}), and a ridge tag from that time reportedly reads: "Tenshō 11 ... invited Hachiman-sha ... shrine official Inagaki Udayū Fujiwara Yasuo ... built by Asano Hachirōzaemon, nephew of Asano Nagamasa, descendant of Genji of Hachiman"{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. However, Edo-period gazetteers do not describe this Hachiman shrine. It was formally merged in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155}}) as a precinct shrine of Wakeoe Shrine{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. The Yamada District "Wakeoe Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is the same as "Ōe Tenjin" in the '{{ill|lt=Owari Kuninai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}.' By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{ill|lt=Owari-shi|Q137855962|WD=Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{ill|lt=old document|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{ill|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843|WD=Q1069843}} during construction of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254|WD=Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

{{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|lt=Hiruko-no-Mikoto}} | 社格 = {{ill|lt=candidate Shikinaisha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建 = Unknown | 別名 = Rokusho Daimyōjin
Rokusho Myōjin
Rokusho-shi | 札所等 = | 本殿 = | 例祭 = October 11 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Nagoya|label=Wakeoe
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{osm box|w|454603831}} '''Wakeoe Shrine''' (わけおえじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Yasui 4-chōme, {{ill|lt=Kita-ku (Nagoya)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Nagoya|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It has been identified with "Wakeoe Shrine" of Yamada District, Owari Province, in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' but there are differing views as described below. === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN}} it was called '''Rokusho Daimyōjin''' and was the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} of Yasui village in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576}}. The early-Edo '{{ill|lt=Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}' records "Yasui village ... Rokusho Daimyōjin ... previously tax-exempt." At that time, the Inagaki family served as {{ill|lt=kannushi|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}}. Founding date is unknown. One theory says it originally stood at Senbonsugi to the northeast and was moved to the current site in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1584|Q6728|WD=Q6728}}) by order of {{ill|lt=Oda Nobukatsu|Q3044815|WD=Q3044815}}, while another says shrine buildings had long stood around the present site because remains of dead Senbonsugi trees are within the precinct{{Sfn|加藤宏|1988|p=281}}. It is said to have lost part of shrine lands due to Bizen land surveys in the {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} period{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. In early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} it was renamed "Wakeoe Shrine." It was burned in the {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}} air raids ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20) but rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1966|Q2649|WD=Q2649}} (Shōwa 41). The Hachiman shrine merged here, also called "Enna Hachiman" or "Engi Hachiman," is said to have originally stood in the same precinct{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. According to shrine tradition, it was founded at Senbonsugi in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenji|Q314802|WD=Q314802}}, but was heavily damaged by flood in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1520|Q6284|WD=Q6284}}){{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. It is said Asano Matazaemon Nagakatsu rebuilt it in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1583|Q6726|WD=Q6726}}), and a ridge tag from that time reportedly reads: "Tenshō 11 ... invited Hachiman-sha ... shrine official Inagaki Udayū Fujiwara Yasuo ... built by Asano Hachirōzaemon, nephew of Asano Nagamasa, descendant of Genji of Hachiman"{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. However, Edo-period gazetteers do not describe this Hachiman shrine. It was formally merged in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155}}) as a precinct shrine of Wakeoe Shrine{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. The Yamada District "Wakeoe Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is the same as "Ōe Tenjin" in the '{{ill|lt=Owari Kuninai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}.' By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{ill|lt=Owari-shi|Q137855962|WD=Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{ill|lt=old document|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{ill|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843|WD=Q1069843}} during construction of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254|WD=Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

It was formally merged in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155}}) as a precinct shrine of Wakeoe Shrine{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. The Yamada District "Wakeoe Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is the same as "Ōe Tenjin" in the '{{ill|lt=Owari Kuninai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}.' By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{ill|lt=Owari-shi|Q137855962|WD=Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{ill|lt=old document|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{ill|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843|WD=Q1069843}} during construction of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254|WD=Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Rokusho-shi | 札所等 = | 本殿 = | 例祭 = October 11 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Nagoya|label=Wakeoe
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{osm box|w|454603831}} '''Wakeoe Shrine''' (わけおえじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Yasui 4-chōme, {{ill|lt=Kita-ku (Nagoya)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Nagoya|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It has been identified with "Wakeoe Shrine" of Yamada District, Owari Province, in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' but there are differing views as described below. === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN}} it was called '''Rokusho Daimyōjin''' and was the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} of Yasui village in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576}}. The early-Edo '{{ill|lt=Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}' records "Yasui village ... Rokusho Daimyōjin ... previously tax-exempt." At that time, the Inagaki family served as {{ill|lt=kannushi|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}}. Founding date is unknown. One theory says it originally stood at Senbonsugi to the northeast and was moved to the current site in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1584|Q6728|WD=Q6728}}) by order of {{ill|lt=Oda Nobukatsu|Q3044815|WD=Q3044815}}, while another says shrine buildings had long stood around the present site because remains of dead Senbonsugi trees are within the precinct{{Sfn|加藤宏|1988|p=281}}. It is said to have lost part of shrine lands due to Bizen land surveys in the {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} period{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. In early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} it was renamed "Wakeoe Shrine." It was burned in the {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}} air raids ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20) but rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1966|Q2649|WD=Q2649}} (Shōwa 41). The Hachiman shrine merged here, also called "Enna Hachiman" or "Engi Hachiman," is said to have originally stood in the same precinct{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. According to shrine tradition, it was founded at Senbonsugi in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenji|Q314802|WD=Q314802}}, but was heavily damaged by flood in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1520|Q6284|WD=Q6284}}){{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. It is said Asano Matazaemon Nagakatsu rebuilt it in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1583|Q6726|WD=Q6726}}), and a ridge tag from that time reportedly reads: "Tenshō 11 ... invited Hachiman-sha ... shrine official Inagaki Udayū Fujiwara Yasuo ... built by Asano Hachirōzaemon, nephew of Asano Nagamasa, descendant of Genji of Hachiman"{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. However, Edo-period gazetteers do not describe this Hachiman shrine. It was formally merged in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155}}) as a precinct shrine of Wakeoe Shrine{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. The Yamada District "Wakeoe Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is the same as "Ōe Tenjin" in the '{{ill|lt=Owari Kuninai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}.' By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{ill|lt=Owari-shi|Q137855962|WD=Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{ill|lt=old document|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{ill|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843|WD=Q1069843}} during construction of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254|WD=Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建 = Unknown | 別名 = Rokusho Daimyōjin
Rokusho Myōjin
Rokusho-shi | 札所等 = | 本殿 = | 例祭 = October 11 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Nagoya|label=Wakeoe
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{osm box|w|454603831}} '''Wakeoe Shrine''' (わけおえじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Yasui 4-chōme, {{ill|lt=Kita-ku (Nagoya)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Nagoya|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It has been identified with "Wakeoe Shrine" of Yamada District, Owari Province, in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' but there are differing views as described below. === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN}} it was called '''Rokusho Daimyōjin''' and was the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} of Yasui village in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576}}. The early-Edo '{{ill|lt=Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}' records "Yasui village ... Rokusho Daimyōjin ... previously tax-exempt." At that time, the Inagaki family served as {{ill|lt=kannushi|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}}. Founding date is unknown. One theory says it originally stood at Senbonsugi to the northeast and was moved to the current site in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1584|Q6728|WD=Q6728}}) by order of {{ill|lt=Oda Nobukatsu|Q3044815|WD=Q3044815}}, while another says shrine buildings had long stood around the present site because remains of dead Senbonsugi trees are within the precinct{{Sfn|加藤宏|1988|p=281}}. It is said to have lost part of shrine lands due to Bizen land surveys in the {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} period{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. In early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} it was renamed "Wakeoe Shrine." It was burned in the {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}} air raids ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20) but rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1966|Q2649|WD=Q2649}} (Shōwa 41). The Hachiman shrine merged here, also called "Enna Hachiman" or "Engi Hachiman," is said to have originally stood in the same precinct{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. According to shrine tradition, it was founded at Senbonsugi in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenji|Q314802|WD=Q314802}}, but was heavily damaged by flood in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1520|Q6284|WD=Q6284}}){{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. It is said Asano Matazaemon Nagakatsu rebuilt it in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1583|Q6726|WD=Q6726}}), and a ridge tag from that time reportedly reads: "Tenshō 11 ... invited Hachiman-sha ... shrine official Inagaki Udayū Fujiwara Yasuo ... built by Asano Hachirōzaemon, nephew of Asano Nagamasa, descendant of Genji of Hachiman"{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. However, Edo-period gazetteers do not describe this Hachiman shrine. It was formally merged in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155}}) as a precinct shrine of Wakeoe Shrine{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. The Yamada District "Wakeoe Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is the same as "Ōe Tenjin" in the '{{ill|lt=Owari Kuninai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}.' By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{ill|lt=Owari-shi|Q137855962|WD=Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{ill|lt=old document|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{ill|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843|WD=Q1069843}} during construction of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254|WD=Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

However, '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{ill|lt=old document|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{ill|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843|WD=Q1069843}} during construction of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254|WD=Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Rokusho-shi | 札所等 = | 本殿 = | 例祭 = October 11 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Nagoya|label=Wakeoe
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{osm box|w|454603831}} '''Wakeoe Shrine''' (わけおえじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Yasui 4-chōme, {{ill|lt=Kita-ku (Nagoya)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Nagoya|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It has been identified with "Wakeoe Shrine" of Yamada District, Owari Province, in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' but there are differing views as described below. === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN}} it was called '''Rokusho Daimyōjin''' and was the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} of Yasui village in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576}}. The early-Edo '{{ill|lt=Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}' records "Yasui village ... Rokusho Daimyōjin ... previously tax-exempt." At that time, the Inagaki family served as {{ill|lt=kannushi|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}}. Founding date is unknown. One theory says it originally stood at Senbonsugi to the northeast and was moved to the current site in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1584|Q6728|WD=Q6728}}) by order of {{ill|lt=Oda Nobukatsu|Q3044815|WD=Q3044815}}, while another says shrine buildings had long stood around the present site because remains of dead Senbonsugi trees are within the precinct{{Sfn|加藤宏|1988|p=281}}. It is said to have lost part of shrine lands due to Bizen land surveys in the {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} period{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. In early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} it was renamed "Wakeoe Shrine." It was burned in the {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}} air raids ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20) but rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1966|Q2649|WD=Q2649}} (Shōwa 41). The Hachiman shrine merged here, also called "Enna Hachiman" or "Engi Hachiman," is said to have originally stood in the same precinct{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. According to shrine tradition, it was founded at Senbonsugi in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenji|Q314802|WD=Q314802}}, but was heavily damaged by flood in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1520|Q6284|WD=Q6284}}){{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. It is said Asano Matazaemon Nagakatsu rebuilt it in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1583|Q6726|WD=Q6726}}), and a ridge tag from that time reportedly reads: "Tenshō 11 ... invited Hachiman-sha ... shrine official Inagaki Udayū Fujiwara Yasuo ... built by Asano Hachirōzaemon, nephew of Asano Nagamasa, descendant of Genji of Hachiman"{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. However, Edo-period gazetteers do not describe this Hachiman shrine. It was formally merged in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155}}) as a precinct shrine of Wakeoe Shrine{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. The Yamada District "Wakeoe Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is the same as "Ōe Tenjin" in the '{{ill|lt=Owari Kuninai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}.' By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{ill|lt=Owari-shi|Q137855962|WD=Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{ill|lt=old document|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{ill|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843|WD=Q1069843}} during construction of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254|WD=Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建 = Unknown | 別名 = Rokusho Daimyōjin
Rokusho Myōjin
Rokusho-shi | 札所等 = | 本殿 = | 例祭 = October 11 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Nagoya|label=Wakeoe
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{osm box|w|454603831}} '''Wakeoe Shrine''' (わけおえじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Yasui 4-chōme, {{ill|lt=Kita-ku (Nagoya)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Nagoya|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It has been identified with "Wakeoe Shrine" of Yamada District, Owari Province, in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' but there are differing views as described below. === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q184963}} it was called '''Rokusho Daimyōjin''' and was the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} of Yasui village in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576}}. The early-Edo '{{ill|lt=Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}' records "Yasui village ... Rokusho Daimyōjin ... previously tax-exempt." At that time, the Inagaki family served as {{ill|lt=kannushi|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}}. Founding date is unknown. One theory says it originally stood at Senbonsugi to the northeast and was moved to the current site in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1584|Q6728|WD=Q6728}}) by order of {{ill|lt=Oda Nobukatsu|Q3044815|WD=Q3044815}}, while another says shrine buildings had long stood around the present site because remains of dead Senbonsugi trees are within the precinct{{Sfn|加藤宏|1988|p=281}}. It is said to have lost part of shrine lands due to Bizen land surveys in the {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} period{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. In early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} it was renamed "Wakeoe Shrine." It was burned in the {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}} air raids ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20) but rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1966|Q2649|WD=Q2649}} (Shōwa 41). The Hachiman shrine merged here, also called "Enna Hachiman" or "Engi Hachiman," is said to have originally stood in the same precinct{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. According to shrine tradition, it was founded at Senbonsugi in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenji|Q314802|WD=Q314802}}, but was heavily damaged by flood in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1520|Q6284|WD=Q6284}}){{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. It is said Asano Matazaemon Nagakatsu rebuilt it in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1583|Q6726|WD=Q6726}}), and a ridge tag from that time reportedly reads: "Tenshō 11 ... invited Hachiman-sha ... shrine official Inagaki Udayū Fujiwara Yasuo ... built by Asano Hachirōzaemon, nephew of Asano Nagamasa, descendant of Genji of Hachiman"{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. However, Edo-period gazetteers do not describe this Hachiman shrine. It was formally merged in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155}}) as a precinct shrine of Wakeoe Shrine{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. The Yamada District "Wakeoe Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is the same as "Ōe Tenjin" in the '{{ill|lt=Owari Kuninai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}.' By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{ill|lt=Owari-shi|Q137855962|WD=Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{ill|lt=old document|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{ill|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843|WD=Q1069843}} during construction of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254|WD=Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{ill|lt=Owari-shi|Q137855962|WD=Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{ill|lt=old document|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{ill|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843|WD=Q1069843}} during construction of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254|WD=Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Japanese content == {{神社 | 画像 = [[File:Wakeoejinja1.JPG|250px]]
Front view of precinct | 名称 = Wakeoe Shrine | 所在地 = {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}{{ill|lt=Kita-ku (Nagoya)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita-ku}}4-14-4 Yasui | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q813858}}

{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|lt=Hiruko-no-Mikoto}} | 社格 = {{ill|lt=candidate Shikinaisha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建 = Unknown | 別名 = Rokusho Daimyōjin
Rokusho Myōjin
Rokusho-shi | 札所等 = | 本殿 = | 例祭 = October 11 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Nagoya|label=Wakeoe
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{osm box|w|454603831}} '''Wakeoe Shrine''' (わけおえじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Yasui 4-chōme, {{ill|lt=Kita-ku (Nagoya)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Nagoya|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It has been identified with "Wakeoe Shrine" of Yamada District, Owari Province, in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' but there are differing views as described below. === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q184963}} it was called '''Rokusho Daimyōjin''' and was the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} of Yasui village in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576}}. The early-Edo '{{ill|lt=Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}' records "Yasui village ... Rokusho Daimyōjin ... previously tax-exempt." At that time, the Inagaki family served as {{ill|lt=kannushi|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}}. Founding date is unknown. One theory says it originally stood at Senbonsugi to the northeast and was moved to the current site in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1584|Q6728|WD=Q6728}}) by order of {{ill|lt=Oda Nobukatsu|Q3044815|WD=Q3044815}}, while another says shrine buildings had long stood around the present site because remains of dead Senbonsugi trees are within the precinct{{Sfn|加藤宏|1988|p=281}}. It is said to have lost part of shrine lands due to Bizen land surveys in the {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} period{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. In early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} it was renamed "Wakeoe Shrine." It was burned in the {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}} air raids ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20) but rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1966|Q2649|WD=Q2649}} (Shōwa 41). The Hachiman shrine merged here, also called "Enna Hachiman" or "Engi Hachiman," is said to have originally stood in the same precinct{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. According to shrine tradition, it was founded at Senbonsugi in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenji|Q314802|WD=Q314802}}, but was heavily damaged by flood in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1520|Q6284|WD=Q6284}}){{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. It is said Asano Matazaemon Nagakatsu rebuilt it in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1583|Q6726|WD=Q6726}}), and a ridge tag from that time reportedly reads: "Tenshō 11 ... invited Hachiman-sha ... shrine official Inagaki Udayū Fujiwara Yasuo ... built by Asano Hachirōzaemon, nephew of Asano Nagamasa, descendant of Genji of Hachiman"{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. However, Edo-period gazetteers do not describe this Hachiman shrine. It was formally merged in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155}}) as a precinct shrine of Wakeoe Shrine{{Sfn|愛知県西春日井郡|1988|p=323}}. The Yamada District "Wakeoe Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is the same as "Ōe Tenjin" in the '{{ill|lt=Owari Kuninai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}.' By the Edo period its location had become unknown; 'Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu no Teikō' states "this shrine's location is unknown now; later generations should examine," and '{{ill|lt=Owari-shi|Q137855962|WD=Q137855962}}' likewise says "Wakeoe Shrine appears in Engi Jinmyōshiki but present location is unclear." However, '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' identified Wakeoe Shrine with Rokusho Myōjin in Yasui village (this shrine). {{Quotation|One theory says it is Suwa Shrine in Kamioda village, Kasugai District, but that is Kasugai District, not Yamada District, and lacking clear origin. In Yasui village, our province's divine-register has Ōe Tenjin, and local names such as Ōeyoshiyama and Senbonsugi provide one basis. Also, in an exemption document given by Asano Katsuyuki, father of Asano Nagamasa, to shrine official Inagaki when he worshiped Ōe Hachiman in this village, it says "Wakeoe Shrine ..." This too is sufficient evidence. For now, append this to reference.|'Tokusen Jinmyōchō,' Wakeoe Shrine}} Thus 'Tokusen Jinmyōchō' identified this shrine based on place names like "Ōeyoshiyama" and the Asano document. But the authenticity of the latter {{ill|lt=old document|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567}} has also been questioned{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%88%A5%E5%B0%8F%E6%B1%9F%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE-3064790|editor=Heibonsha|website=Kotobank|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei "Wakeoe Shrine"|accessdate=2025-11-06}}. Also, the 1143 ({{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459}} 2) "Owari no Kuni Ajiki-shō Rikkenmon" lists "Yasui-ri" as a village-name within Ajiki-shō of Harube District, so Yasui is considered likely to have belonged to Harube, not Yamada, in the Heian period{{Sfn|「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|1989|p=1350}}. === Notes === Long revered as deity of safe childbirth, children's protection, and longevity by figures such as {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|Q32613|WD=Q32613}}, Tameyoshi-go, and successive Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa leaders. The "Meoto-sugi" pair in the precinct is a natural monument and is said to be a city historic scenic site. Stones used for Kiyomasa Bridge, set on the Inaoki Road by {{ill|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|Q1069843|WD=Q1069843}} during construction of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}}, are said to remain in the precinct. === Precinct shrines === * Hachiman-sha * Hi-sha * Kotohira-sha * Ontake-sha * Tsushima-sha === Gallery === {{gallery |File:Wakeoejinja2.JPG|Haiden |File:Wakeoejinja3.JPG|Kiyomasa stone }} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= {{Cite web|和書|url=http://wakeoe.com/html/gaiyou.html|title=About this shrine|publisher=Wakeoe Shrine|accessdate=2019-01-13|language=日本語}} }} === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Aichi Prefecture|Q30925254|WD=Q30925254}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} * {{Official}}{{Ja icon}} * [https://www.instagram.com/wakeoeshrine Wakeoe Shrine] (Instagram) {{DEFAULTSORT:わけおえしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakeoe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:名古屋市の神社]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧村社]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の歴史]] [[Category:名古屋市北区の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]
